title: String Methods
summary: Learn how to transform, search, and clean text using Python's built-in string methods — the everyday tools every Python programmer reaches for.

blocks.md: Strings are everywhere: names, messages, addresses, emails. Real-world text is messy — it has extra spaces, odd capitalization, or needs to be split apart. Python gives every string a built-in toolkit called **methods**. A method is like a helper function that lives *on* the string itself. You call it with a dot: `"hello".upper()`. In this lesson you will learn the most useful ones.
Three methods handle capitalization. They are useful for displaying names nicely or comparing text without worrying about whether someone typed 'PYTHON' or 'python'.

blocks.code:
name = "alice wonder"

print(name.upper())   # all capitals
print(name.lower())   # all lowercase
print(name.title())   # first letter of each word capitalised
blocks.output:
ALICE WONDER
alice wonder
Alice Wonder
blocks.caption: upper(), lower(), and title() in action

blocks.title: Methods are tools on a Swiss Army knife
blocks.md: Think of a string as a piece of paper, and methods as the tools clipped to the side. You pick the tool you need — `upper()`, `lower()`, `strip()` — and it does its job, handing you back a *new* piece of paper with the result.

blocks.text: Strings Are Immutable — Methods Return NEW Strings

blocks.md: This is one of the most important things to understand: **calling a method does not change the original string**. It always hands you back a brand-new string. You need to save that result in a variable if you want to use it.

blocks.code:
greeting = "hello"

# This does NOT change greeting
greeting.upper()

print(greeting)  # still lowercase!

# You must capture the result
louder = greeting.upper()
print(louder)
blocks.caption: The original string is never changed

blocks.title: Forgetting to save the result
blocks.md:
A very common beginner mistake:

```python
word = "python"
word.upper()        # result is thrown away!
print(word)         # still 'python'
```

Always assign the result: `word = word.upper()`.
When users type text into a form, they often accidentally add spaces at the start or end. `strip()` removes those for you. `lstrip()` removes only from the left, and `rstrip()` only from the right.

blocks.code:
user_input = "   alice@example.com   "

cleaned = user_input.strip()
print(repr(cleaned))  # repr() shows quotes so you can see spaces clearly
blocks.caption: strip() removes leading and trailing whitespace

blocks.md: `replace(old, new)` swaps every occurrence of one piece of text with another. `find(text)` tells you *where* something appears — it returns the index (position number), or `-1` if not found.

blocks.code:
sentence = "I love cats. Cats are great."

print(sentence.replace("cats", "dogs"))
print(sentence.replace("Cats", "Dogs"))
blocks.output:
I love dogs. Cats are great.
I love cats. Dogs are great.
blocks.caption: replace() is case-sensitive — 'cats' and 'Cats' are treated differently

blocks.code:
text = "Python is fun"

position = text.find("fun")
print(position)   # index where 'fun' starts

missing = text.find("boring")
print(missing)    # -1 means not found
blocks.caption: find() returns the starting index, or -1 if absent

blocks.md: `count(text)` tells you how many times something appears. `startswith(text)` and `endswith(text)` check the beginning or end of a string and return `True` or `False`. `isdigit()` checks whether every character is a number.

blocks.code:
sentence = "banana banana banana"

print(sentence.count("banana"))       # how many times?
print(sentence.startswith("banana"))  # True or False?
print(sentence.endswith("apple"))     # True or False?

code = "12345"
print(code.isdigit())  # True — all digits
blocks.caption: Counting and checking methods

blocks.text: Splitting and Joining — A Power Pair

blocks.md: `split()` breaks a string into a **list** of pieces. `join()` does the reverse — it glues a list of strings back together. They work perfectly as a team for reshaping text.

blocks.code:
csv_line = "Alice,Bob,Charlie,Diana"

names = csv_line.split(",")  # split on the comma
print(names)
print(type(names))
blocks.caption: split() turns a string into a list

blocks.code:
names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"]

# join() is called on the SEPARATOR, not the list
result = " | ".join(names)
print(result)
blocks.caption: join() is called on the separator string — this trips up many beginners!

blocks.title: split() with no argument splits on any whitespace
blocks.md: `"  hello   world  ".split()` returns `['hello', 'world']` — it automatically handles multiple spaces and strips them. Very handy for cleaning up messy input.
Because each method returns a new string, you can call another method immediately on that result. This is called **chaining** — reading left to right, each method hands its output to the next.

blocks.code:
raw = "   HELLO, world!   "

# strip, then lower, then replace — all in one line
cleaned = raw.strip().lower().replace(",", "")
print(cleaned)
blocks.caption: Chaining: strip() -> lower() -> replace()

blocks.text: A Practical Example: Cleaning User Input

blocks.md: Here is how you might combine several methods to clean up a username before saving it to a database.

blocks.code:
raw_username = "  Ali Khan  "

username = raw_username.strip().lower().replace(" ", "_")
print(username)
blocks.caption: strip whitespace, lowercase, replace spaces with underscores

blocks.question:
What does this code print?

```python
word = "python"
word.upper()
print(word)
```
blocks.explanation: String methods never modify the original string — they return a NEW string. Because the result of word.upper() was never saved, 'word' still holds the original lowercase 'python'.

keyTakeaways: String methods are called with a dot: `text.method()` — they are built right into every string.
Methods never change the original string; they always return a new one. Always save the result.
`strip()`, `replace()`, and `lower()` are your best friends for cleaning messy text.
`split()` turns a string into a list; `join()` turns a list back into a string — use them as a pair.
You can chain methods one after another to transform text in a single readable line.

exercise.prompt: You have received a messy list of email addresses as a single string: `"  Alice@Example.COM , bob@GMAIL.COM,  CHARLIE@outlook.com  "`. Your task: split it on the comma, then clean each email by stripping whitespace and converting to lowercase. Print each cleaned email on its own line.
exercise.starterCode:
raw = "  Alice@Example.COM , bob@GMAIL.COM,  CHARLIE@outlook.com  "

# Split on comma first
emails = raw.split(",")

# Loop through and clean each one
for email in emails:
    cleaned = email  # TODO: chain .strip() and .lower() to clean the email
    print(cleaned)
exercise.hint: Chain .strip() and .lower() on each email inside the loop.
exercise.solution:
raw = "  Alice@Example.COM , bob@GMAIL.COM,  CHARLIE@outlook.com  "

emails = raw.split(",")

for email in emails:
    cleaned = email.strip().lower()
    print(cleaned)
